sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: ἰοὺ ἰού ·
sections.translation: Ah me ! Ah me ! O King Jupiter , of what a terrible length the nights are ! Will it never be day ? And yet long since I heard the cock .

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: οἱ δʼ οἰκέται ῥέγκουσιν · ἀλλʼ οὐκ ἂν πρὸ τοῦ .
sections.translation: My domestics are snoring ; but they would not have done so heretofore ! May you perish then , O war ! For many reasons ; because I may not even punish my domestics . Neither does this excellent youth awake through the night ; but takes his ease ,

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: ἐν πέντε σισύραις ἐγκεκορδυλημένος .
sections.translation: wrapped up in five blankets . Well , if it is the fashion , let us snore wrapped up .

sections.label: Section 12
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐ δύναμαι δείλαιος εὕδειν δακνόμενος
sections.translation: But I am not able , miserable man , to sleep , being tormented by my expenses , and my stud of horses , and my debts , through this son of mine . He with his long hair ,

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: ἱππάζεταί τε καὶ ξυνωρικεύεται
sections.translation: is riding horses and driving curricles , and dreaming of horses ; while I am driven to distraction , as I see the moon bringing on the twentieths ; for the interest is running on . Boy ! Light a lamp , and bring forth my tablets , that I may take them and read

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: ὁπόσοις ὀφείλω καὶ λογίσωμαι τοὺς τόκους .
sections.translation: to how many I am indebted , and calculate the interest . Enter boy with a light and tablets . Come , let me see ; what do I owe ? Twelve minae to Pasias . Why twelve minae to Pasias ? Why did I borrow them ? When I bought the blood-horse . Ah me , unhappy ! Would that it had had its eye knocked out with a stone first !

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: Φίλων ἀδικεῖς · ἔλαυνε τὸν σαυτοῦ δρόμον .
sections.translation: You are acting unfairly , Philo ! Drive on your own course .

sections.label: Section 26
sections.original: τοῦτʼ ἔστι τουτὶ τὸ κακὸν ὅ μʼ ἀπολώλεκεν ·
sections.translation: This is the bane that has destroyed me ; for even in his sleep he dreams about horsemanship .

sections.label: Section 28
sections.original: πόσους δρόμους ἐλᾷ τὰ πολεμιστήρια ;
sections.translation: How many courses will the war-chariots run ?

sections.label: Section 29
sections.original: ἐμὲ μὲν σὺ πολλοὺς τὸν πατέρʼ ἐλαύνεις δρόμους .
sections.translation: Many courses do you drive me , your father . But what debt came upon me after Pasias ? Three minae to Amynias for a little chariot and pair of wheels .

sections.label: Section 32
sections.original: ἄπαγε τὸν ἵππον ἐξαλίσας οἴκαδε .
sections.translation: Lead the horse home , after having given him a good rolling .

sections.label: Section 33
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὦ μέλʼ ἐξήλικας ἐμέ γʼ ἐκ τῶν ἐμῶν ,
sections.translation: O foolish youth , you have rolled me out of my possessions ; since I have been cast in suits , and others say that they will have surety given them for the interest .

sections.label: Section 35b
sections.original: ἐτεὸν ὦ πάτερ
sections.translation: Pray , father , why are you peevish , and toss about the whole night ?

sections.label: Section 37
sections.original: δάκνει μὲ δήμαρχός τις ἐκ τῶν στρωμάτων .
sections.translation: A bailiff out of the bedclothes is biting me .

sections.label: Section 38
sections.original: ἔασον ὦ δαιμόνιε καταδαρθεῖν τί με .
sections.translation: Suffer me , good sir , to sleep a little .

sections.label: Section 39
sections.original: σὺ δʼ οὖν κάθευδε · τὰ δὲ χρέα ταῦτʼ ἴσθʼ ὅτι
sections.translation: Then , do you sleep on ; but know that all these debts will turn on your head . Phidippides falls asleep again . Alas ! Would that the match-maker had perished miserably , who induced me to marry your mother . For a country life used to be most agreeable to me , dirty , untrimmed , reclining at random ,

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: βρύων μελίτταις καὶ προβάτοις καὶ στεμφύλοις .
sections.translation: abounding in bees , and sheep , and oil-cake . Then I , a rustic , married a niece of Megacles , the son of Megacles , from the city , haughty , luxurious , and Coesyrafied . When I married her , I lay with her

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: ὄζων τρυγὸς τρασιᾶς ἐρίων περιουσίας ,
sections.translation: redolent of new wine , of the cheese-crate , and abundance of wool ; but she , on the contrary , of ointment , saffron , wanton-kisses , extravagance , gluttony , and of Colias and Genetyllis . I will not indeed say that she was idle ; but she wove . And I used to show her this cloak

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: πρόφασιν ἔφασκον , ὦ γύναι λίαν σπαθᾷς .
sections.translation: by way of a pretext and say Wife , you weave at a great rate .

sections.label: Section 56
sections.original: ἔλαιον ἡμῖν οὐκ ἔνεστʼ ἐν τῷ λύχνῳ .
sections.translation: We have no oil in the lamp .

sections.label: Section 57
sections.original: οἴμοι · τί γάρ μοι τὸν πότην ἧπτες λύχνον ;
sections.translation: Ah me ! Why did you light the thirsty lamp ? Come hither that you may weep !

sections.label: Section 58b
sections.original: διὰ τί δῆτα κλαύσομαι ;
sections.translation: For what , pray , shall I weep ?

sections.label: Section 59
sections.original: ὅτι τῶν παχειῶν ἐνετίθεις θρυαλλίδων .
sections.translation: Because you put in one of the thick wicks .

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: μετὰ ταῦθʼ , ὅπως νῷν ἐγένεθʼ υἱὸς οὑτοσί ,
sections.translation: After this , when this son was born to us , to me , forsooth , and to my excellent wife , we squabbled then about the name : for she was for adding hippos to the name , Xanthippus , or Charippus , or Callipides ;

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: ἐγὼ δὲ τοῦ πάππου ʼτιθέμην Φειδωνίδην .
sections.translation: but I was for giving him the name of his grandfather , Phidonides . For a time therefore we disputed ; and then at length we agreed , and called him Phidippides . She used to take this son and fondle him , saying , When you , being grown up , shall drive your chariot to the city ,

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: ὥσπερ Μεγακλέης , ξυστίδʼ ἔχων . ἐγὼ δʼ ἔφην ,
sections.translation: like Megacles , with a xystis . But I used to say , Nay , rather , when dressed in a leathern jerkin , you shall drive goats from Phelleus , like your father . He paid no attention to my words , but poured a horse-fever over my property .

sections.label: Section 75
sections.original: νῦν οὖν ὅλην τὴν νύκτα φροντίζων ὁδοῦ
sections.translation: Now , therefore , by meditating the whole night , I have discovered one path for my course extraordinarily excellent ; to which if I persuade this youth I shall be saved . But first I wish to awake him . How then can I awake him in the most agreeable manner ? How ?

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: Φειδιππίδη Φειδιππίδιον .
sections.translation: Phidippides , my little Phidippides ?

sections.label: Section 80b
sections.original: τί ὦ πάτερ ;
sections.translation: What , father ?

sections.label: Section 81
sections.original: κύσον με καὶ τὴν χεῖρα δὸς τὴν δεξιάν .
sections.translation: Kiss me , and give me your right hand !

sections.label: Section 82
sections.original: ἰδού . τί ἔστιν ;
sections.translation: There . What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 82b
sections.original: εἰπέ μοι , φιλεῖς ἐμέ ;
sections.translation: Tell me , do you love me ?

sections.label: Section 83
sections.original: νὴ τὸν Ποσειδῶ τουτονὶ τὸν ἵππιον .
sections.translation: Yes , by this Equestrian Neptune .

sections.label: Section 84
sections.original: μὴ ʼμοί γε τοῦτον μηδαμῶς τὸν ἵππιον ·
sections.translation: Nay , do not by any means mention this Equestrian to me , for this god is the author of my misfortunes . But , if you really love me from your heart , my son , obey me .

sections.label: Section 87b
sections.original: τί οὖν πίθωμαι δῆτά σοι ;
sections.translation: In what then , pray , shall I obey you ?

sections.label: Section 88
sections.original: ἔκστρεψον ὡς τάχιστα τοὺς σαυτοῦ τρόπους ,
sections.translation: Reform your habits as quickly as possible , and go and learn what I advise .

sections.label: Section 90
sections.original: λέγε δή , τί κελεύεις ;
sections.translation: Tell me now , what do you prescribe ?

sections.label: Section 90b
sections.original: καί τι πείσει ;
sections.translation: And will you obey me at all ?

sections.label: Section 90c
sections.original: πείσομαι
sections.translation: By Bacchus , I will obey you .

sections.label: Section 91b
sections.original: δεῦρό νυν ἀπόβλεπε .
sections.translation: Look this way then ! Do you see this little door and little house ?

sections.label: Section 93
sections.original: ὁρῶ . τί οὖν τοῦτʼ ἐστὶν ἐτεὸν ὦ πάτερ ;
sections.translation: I see it . What then , pray , is this , father ?

sections.label: Section 94
sections.original: ψυχῶν σοφῶν τοῦτʼ ἐστὶ φροντιστήριον .
sections.translation: This is a thinking-shop of wise spirits .

sections.label: Section 95
sections.original: ἐνταῦθʼ ἐνοικοῦσʼ ἄνδρες , οἳ τὸν οὐρανὸν
sections.translation: There dwell men who in speaking of the heavens persuade people that it is an oven , and that it encompasses us , and that we are the embers . These men teach , if one give them money , to conquer in speaking , right or wrong .

sections.label: Section 100
sections.original: εἰσὶν δὲ τίνες ;
sections.translation: Who are they ?

sections.label: Section 100b
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ ἀκριβῶς τοὔνομα ·
sections.translation: I do not know the name accurately . They are minute philosophers , noble and excellent .

sections.label: Section 103
sections.original: αἰβοῖ πονηροί γʼ , οἶδα . τοὺς ἀλαζόνας
sections.translation: Bah ! They are rogues ; I know them . You mean the quacks , the pale-faced wretches , the bare-footed fellows , of whose numbers are the miserable Socrates and Chaerephon .

sections.label: Section 105
sections.original: ὧν ὁ κακοδαίμων Σωκράτης καὶ Χαιρεφῶν .
sections.translation: Hold ! Hold ! Be silent ! Do not say anything foolish . But , if you have any concern for your father ’ s patrimony , become one of them , having given up your horsemanship .

sections.label: Section 108
sections.original: οὐκ ἂν μὰ τὸν Διόνυσον , εἰ δοίης γέ μοι
sections.translation: I would not , by Bacchus , even if you were to give me the pheasants which Leogoras rears !

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: ἴθʼ ἀντιβολῶ σʼ ὦ φίλτατʼ ἀνθρώπων ἐμοὶ
sections.translation: Go , I entreat you , dearest of men , go and be taught .

sections.label: Section 111b
sections.original: καὶ τί σοι μαθήσομαι ;
sections.translation: Why , what shall I learn ?

sections.label: Section 112
sections.original: εἶναι παρʼ αὐτοῖς φασιν ἄμφω τὼ λόγω ,
sections.translation: They say that among them are both the two causes — the better cause , whichever that is , and the worse : they say that the one of these two causes , the worse , prevails ,

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: νικᾶν λέγοντά φασι τἀδικώτερα .
sections.translation: though it speaks on the unjust side . If , therefore you learn for me this unjust cause , I would not pay any one , not even an obolus of these debts , which I owe at present on your account .

sections.label: Section 119
sections.original: οὐκ ἂν πιθοίμην · οὐ γὰρ ἂν τλαίην ἰδεῖν
sections.translation: I can not comply ; for I should not dare to look upon the knights , having lost all my colour .

sections.label: Section 121
sections.original: οὐκ ἄρα μὰ τὴν Δήμητρα τῶν γʼ ἐμῶν ἔδει ,
sections.translation: Then , by Ceres , you shall not eat any of my good ! Neither you , nor your blood-horse ; but I will drive you out of my house to the crows .

sections.label: Section 124
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐ περιόψεταί μʼ ὁ θεῖος Μεγακλέης
sections.translation: My uncle Megacles will not permit me to be without a horse . But I ’ ll go in , and pay no heed to you .

sections.label: Section 126
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐδʼ ἐγὼ μέντοι πεσών γε κείσομαι ,
sections.translation: Though fallen , still I will not lie prostrate : but having prayed to the gods , I will go myself to the thinking-shop and get taught . How , then , being an old man ,

sections.label: Section 130
sections.original: λόγων ἀκριβῶν σχινδαλάμους μαθήσομαι ;
sections.translation: shall I learn the subtleties of refined disquisitions ? I must go . Why thus do I loiter and not knock at the door ? Knocks at the door . Boy ! Little boy !

sections.label: Section 133
sections.original: βάλλʼ ἐς κόρακας · τίς ἐσθʼ ὁ κόψας τὴν θύραν ;
sections.translation: Go to the devil ! Who it is that knocked at the door ?

sections.label: Section 134
sections.original: Φείδωνος υἱὸς Στρεψιάδης Κικυννόθεν .
sections.translation: Strepsiades , the son of Phidon , of Cicynna .

sections.label: Section 135
sections.original: ἀμαθής γε νὴ Δίʼ ὅστις οὑτωσὶ σφόδρα
sections.translation: You are a stupid fellow , by Jove ! who have kicked against the door so very carelessly , and have caused the miscarriage of an idea which I had conceived .
